headlines.title: Firefighters battle brush fire in Antioch
headlines.description: Firefighters are battling a wildfire in Antioch Friday that has burned over 10 acres, according to authorities. The incident, dubbed the Deer Fire, started just after 2:30 pm. in the area of Deer Valley Road and Deer Hill Lane, according to the Cal Fire website. The fire was burning 30 acres, but it later shrunk to 15 acres, according to authorities. Fire officials said some strcutures were threatened at one point, but they are no longer in danger. At around 10:30 p.m, officials said the forward progress stopped. The cause of the fire is unknown.

headlines.title: Fans excited ahead of U.S.-Bosnia and Herzegovina match in Santa Clara
headlines.description: U.S. soccer fans are expected to bring a lot of business and enthusiasm to the Bay Area next week, as the USMNT takes on Bosnia and Herzegovina in the Round of 32 of the World Cup. Watch parties for the July 1 game are already planned for fans on both sides of the match; one of those will be hosted by the American Outlaws soccer group at Jack’s Bar and Lounge in San Jose. The American Outlaws have also been helping get shuttles for their members to the games and with booking hotels. They say hotels and shuttle reservations are filling up fast, and they will march to the stadium the day of the game. This is just a small sample of what the American Outlaws expect for the July 1 watch party; a surreal experience for one of the organizers, including Jordan Trigg, a long-time U.S. soccer fan who helped form a local “American Outlaws” chapter in San Jose a decade ago. “To see it all come to fruition and actually and have the US team playing here next week – it’s once in a lifetime,” Trigg said. Trigg says he’ll be at the game with his family, and will be with the American Outlaw team USA fans when they perform their traditional march into the stadium. “The march is going to be chanting the whole time, scarfs waving, collecting people along the way – and it’s just going to get that enthusiasm going,” he said. Bosnia-Herzegovina fans say just being here is a win. “I love it. We already win. We have nothing to lose,” Bosnia fan Ferid Tabakovic said. “I go to every game. I already bought tickets for Santa Clara,” Bosnia fan Sead Tucakovic said. They’re not expected to attract nearly as many fans to the bay area for the game, but they will bring plenty of enthusiasm to the Santa Clara stadium and to watch parties. “It means much more to us than just a game, because we are a small country, we went through hell, during the war, and now this just freedom for us to show love for the countries,” Tucakovic said. Santa Clara’s Euro Grill is planning a special menu for a watch party and offering courtesy drinks to fans. “I’m hoping that at least a small part of them will find their way to our restaurant, so we can share that joy,” Mirza Avviz with Euro Grill said. The owners proudly display mementos from the Sarajevo Olympics and their team’s colors, but they also say they will not be rooting against the U.S. team, from their second home. “People who live here are proud to be part of both countries. Of course, Bosnia Herzegovina that’s our Mother Land, but this is our country right now,” Avviz said. Some of the fans from both teams have been traveling across North America to see their teams play. And this is a home game for those fans from the Bay Area, so all of these watch parties should be held on July 1. The Golden Gate Restaurant Association also said they are helping to set up more watch parties for The American Outlaws and Mexico and Argentina fans.

headlines.title: Privacy group issues safety advisory over ID scanners at San Francisco gay bars
headlines.description: A national digital privacy group has issued a safety advisory ahead of Pride weekend, warning patrons about ID-scanning kiosks used at several popular nightclubs in San Francisco’s Castro neighborhood. The devices, manufactured by the Canadian company Patronscan, require door staff to scan a customer’s ID and take a digital photograph before allowing entry. The kiosks are currently in use at Badlands, Toad Hall, and The Mix. While advocates argue the technology poses a significant risk to the LGBTQIA+ community by unnecessarily tracking personal data, bar management insists the scanners are vital tools to keep patrons and staff safe following a violent attack last year. Fight for the Future, a digital rights advocacy group, issued the advisory specifically naming the three Castro bars and urging Pride weekend attendees to make informed choices about where they celebrate. “It’s really not a safe time to start making databases of queer people that could be accessed by the Trump administration, by a private company, by a bad actor,” said Evan Greer with Fight for the Future. Greer noted that similar privacy concerns surrounding Patronscan have been raised at other major venues, including Madison Square Garden in New York. Eric Norman, a frequent patron of The Mix, said his initial annoyance with the scanning process evolved into genuine concern as he learned more about the technology and its potential implications. “This is way more than just verifying my ID,” Norman said. “The more I learned, it went from me being annoyed to an actual concern, and feeling like we shouldn’t have this technology in use.” Safety Measures and Company Response Despite the pushback, the general manager of The Mix, identified only as Nick, vigorously defended the technology. He said the club installed the system after a customer followed an employee after their shift and brutally attacked them near the Castro Theatre last April. “It keeps my customers safe. We’ve had no bar fights, vandalism and theft is down,” the manager said. He added that the bar would drop the product immediately if it found out the company was sharing data with a third party. In a detailed statement and blog post, a Patronscan spokesperson pushed back against the privacy claims, stating their system does not use facial recognition or collect biometric data. Furthermore, the company stated that to comply with state law, it deletes all ID and photo data in California after 21 days. While the debate over the technology’s place in the Castro continues, patrons like Norman are calling for open dialogue with business owners and city government rather than organizing protests. “I would never encourage anyone to not go into a bar,” Norman said. “I would encourage people to politely articulate, ‘I have a problem with this software.'”

headlines.title: Chemical odor prompts shelter-in-place order in Walnut Creek
headlines.description: A chemical odor in Walnut Creek prompted a shelter-in-place order Friday, according to authorities. The Contra Costa County Fire Protection District said the incident was reported at 4:48 p.m. at 2020 Gill Port Lane in Walnut Creek. The incident was previously reported as a hazardous chemical spill, but fire officials later clarified that a chemical odor was what triggered the alert, which has since been lifted.

headlines.title: Vandal shatters windows at Vallejo gay bar 2 nights in a row
headlines.description: Solano County’s only gay bar was vandalized on consecutive nights this week, causing thousands of dollars in damage in what the owners believe were targeted attacks ahead of regional Pride events. Surveillance cameras captured a masked suspect in a hooded sweatshirt using a large metal padlock to smash the front glass door and windows at Town House Cocktail Lounge shortly after the bar closed at 1 a.m. Thursday. Nobody was inside at the time, and the owners decided to leave the padlock in the window to avoid disturbing the scene. The destruction continued early Friday morning when someone — possibly the same person — shattered the side windows of a neighboring storefront rented out by the bar as a leather workshop, where Pride flags were draped inside. “We actually heard the guy last night,” said Jeff Linn, who co-owns the lounge with his partner, Andy Pembroke, and lives nearby. “I threw on my clothes as quickly as possible and ran downstairs and raced outside and screamed at him.” Vallejo police are investigating the back-to-back attacks. Vallejo Police Sgt. Rashad Hollis confirmed the investigation but said as of now, “there are no elements of a hate crime.” Linn said he feels safe in Vallejo and hopes the incident is just petty crime, but both owners suspect the business was targeted because it is a gay bar. “I hate to call it a hate crime, but it feels like it and it feels like there’s a lot of intent,” Linn said. Pembroke and Linn bought the bar in 2020 and opened it in 2021. Since then, the business has grown exponentially, drawing crowds for karaoke and drag nights and becoming a safe space for the local LGBTQIA+ community. “There are a lot of Pride events going on, so that could be their frame of mind,” Pembroke said. Despite the attacks, the owners are determined to keep their doors open. “I don’t wanna give into what they’re trying to accomplish,” Pembroke said. “They wanna smash our windows, all right, we’ll wake up, clean up the glass, move on.” Linn estimated the window repairs will cost about $4,500. A GoFundMe campaign established to help the business cover the costs had raised more than $2,200 as of Friday afternoon. The owners had planned to attend Pride events around the Bay Area this weekend, including San Francisco’s annual Pride parade, which draws tens of thousands of people to celebrate LGBTQIA+ life. “I don’t know if we will go now,” Linn said.
headlines.source: Jodi Hernandez and Bay City News | Griffin Jones

headlines.title: Remains of more than 100 dogs found at mass grave site in Northern California
headlines.description: More than 100 intact dogs in various stages of decomposition were found at buildings and grounds associated with Miranda’s Rescue Animal Sanctuary, the Humboldt Sheriff’s Office said on Friday. The sheriff’s office said they obtained a search warrant to search the grounds and buildings associated with Miranda’s Rescue and Shannon Miranda, for evidence related to fraud and animal cruelty on Thursday. The warrant allowed the sheriff’s office to excavate open fields for evidence of deceased dogs believed to be buried in mass graves, according to officials. Sheriff’s officials reported investigators found 117 intact canine remains from two dig sites. Near that site, authorities said 21 canine skulls, hundreds of bones, and six loose microchips were also found. According to authorities, the 117 intact dogs were in various stages of decomposition. Officials added most of the dogs were microchipped. The USDA and forensic veterinarians examined 70 of those dogs, determining the cause of death for most of them was gunshot wounds, authorities said. They added the other 47 dogs were not examined due to time constraints. The sheriff’s office said they also found more dead canines in advance stage of decomposition. Investigators tried to remove the animals from the location, but due to their advance stage of decomposition, authorities said it was determined that the evidentiary value of removing the animals from the ground was not justified. Officials said they also found a barn believed to be where the dogs were likely killed. There, the sheriff’s office reported investigators also found 600 dog collars. The Humboldt County Sheriff’s Office noted that, due to the complexity of the investigation, the evidence review process might take a while. After all evidence is reviewed, they said if there is sufficient evidence to support violations of animal cruelty, fraud or other applicable laws, the case will be submitted to the persecution team for review.

headlines.title: Oakland Unified approves tentative budget for next school year
headlines.description: After facing a more than $100 million budget deficit and months of financial uncertainty, the Oakland Unified School District has approved a tentative budget for next school year. District leaders said they’ve cut the deficit down to a $30 million structural deficit while still projecting a nearly $8 million balance. Those numbers have left some board members and the public confused. “It’s impossible to have a deficit and a surplus at the same time,” school board member Mike Hutchinson said during a school board meeting Wednesday. Jennifer Brouhard, president of the school board, said the district is taking a new approach by prioritizing using its reserve funds. The board still has to identify $30 million in cuts, though that figure could drop depending on the state budget. “We do have the $7.9 million in our balance and we’ve also met our state requirement for our reserves and our adopted board requirement, which is higher, and we are also able to make all our payroll,” Brouhard said. The Alameda County superintendent recently raised concerns about the district’s fiscal stability. The Oakland NAACP and other community members are calling for more transparency, saying without a clear plan, future budget cuts could impact students. “We know that there are financial ways – taking from Paul to pay Peter and getting through the moment – but what we’re asking, one, clear numbers and that there be a sustainability plan,” NAACP spokesperson and former school board member Jorge Lerma said. “We want to know what comes after this.” Brouhard said the tentative budget includes the $32 million increase for teachers’ contracts approved to avert a strike. She said the district worked with an independent auditor on the numbers and agreed more transparency is needed. The school board returns in August, at which time members will look to identify the numbers to cut. “The figures are there, the money is there,” Brouhard said. “To have a budget that’s balanced for the first time in 22 years is something that we should be celebrating, and it’s a budget that kept us out of receivership.”

headlines.title: Sharks select Swedish forward Ivar Stenberg at No. 2 overall in 2026 NHL Draft
headlines.description: The Sharks got their guy. San Jose used its first of three scheduled first-round picks in the 2026 NHL Draft to select Swedish forward Ivar Stenberg at No. 2 overall, bringing a young-but-experienced talent to Team Teal. The Toronto Maple Leafs unsurprisingly used the No. 1 selection on Gavin McKenna. From Sweden to San Jose. 📍 pic.twitter.com/AUabkEHtIp — San Jose Sharks (@SanJoseSharks) June 26, 2026 Regarded as one of the most intelligent, NHL-ready players in the draft, Stenberg will turn 19 in September and has been playing professionally in Sweden with the Swedish Elite League’s Frölunda HC. During the 2025-26 campaign, Stenberg finished with 11 goals and 33 points in 43 games — the fifth-most points recorded by an 18-year-old in SEL history. “Decision-making is what stands out with this kid,” Frölunda coach Robert Ohlsson told The Athletic’s Scott Powers and Eric Stephens this week. “He can really read a game. He can play it in slow tempo. I would say, he can control the tempo. [There are] maybe not so many players who can control the temple like he does. He can like lower it a little bit and then he can go uptempo and stuff like that. … He reads the situation before he goes into the situation. That’s maybe what stands out most with him. He never really gets hit so much or never has his eyes down on the ice. He always scanning, scanning and read where he’s got his teammates and where he’s got the opponents.” First SHL goal for 2026 NHL Draft Eligible, Ivar Stenberg. Filthy redirection from the top of the circle. Will be interesting to see the role he will play for Frolunda in the playoffs #Frolunda #FrolundaHC #IvarStenberg #Sweden #Svensk #NHLDraft pic.twitter.com/CU1vW580jZ — ScorchSupremacy (@ScorchSupremacy) March 12, 2025 The Sharks’ trade of William Eklund to the Ottawa Senators on Tuesday opened the door for general manager Mike Grier to select another forward in Stenberg — and also brought the No. 9 overall selection in this year’s draft to San Jose, which many predict Grier will use on a defenseman if not packaged in a deal for a proven vet. Stenberg joins a youthful group of offensive talent on the Sharks, including 2023’s No. 4 pick Will Smith, 2024’s No. 1 overall selection Macklin Celebrini and last year’s No. 2 pick Michael Misa. The future is NOW 🦈 pic.twitter.com/gNxgPWo6Es — Sharks on NBCS (@NBCSSharks) June 26, 2026 With the addition of Stenberg, the future certainly is now.

headlines.title: Brush fire breaks out in Communications Hill area of San Jose
headlines.description: A brush fire broke out in the Communications Hill area of San Jose late Friday morning. The blaze reportedly burned in the area of Communications Hill Boulevard and Monte Vista Drive. The San Jose Fire Department said roughly an acre burned. Additional information wasn’t immediately available. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Former executive sues Meta over attempts to ‘silence' her memoir, ‘Careless People'
headlines.description: A former Meta executive whose memoir, “Careless People,” provides an explosive insider account of her time at the social media giant, has sued the company for attempting to “silence” her. The lawsuit, filed Thursday in federal court in Northern California, claims the tech giant’s private arbitration order barring her from speaking about the company or promoting her bestselling book is invalid. It also argues that the severance agreement she signed when she left Meta, in which she agreed not to disparage the company, was done under duress. Sarah Wynn-Williams served as director of global public policy at Facebook, now operating under parent company Meta Platforms Inc., from 2011 until her firing in 2017. “Careless People” alleges cruel and otherwise disturbing behavior by CEO Mark Zuckerberg and other executives. It also describes Zuckerberg’s alleged efforts to win favor with Chinese officials. Meta has countered that Wynn-Williams violated her agreement and wrote a book filled with inaccuracies. The lawsuit says Meta is seeking $50,000 in damages for each time Wynn-Williams purportedly violates the non-disparagement agreement, putting her under financial duress. She is asking the court to lift the arbitration order and vacate her severance agreement with the company. Meta said in a statement that its “former employee is trying to use the legal process to sell books, which an arbitrator already ruled broke the agreement she signed with the company when she accepted a large severance payment years ago. Her book is divorced from reality, disparaging and riddled with false claims.” Meta, according to the lawsuit, had obtained an emergency gag order that bars Wynn-Williams and her lawyers from criticizing the company or promoting her book. Over the course of more than a year since the book was published, the lawsuit claims, Meta has surveilled her, with company representatives attending her public appearances and photographing her, “all to document that at each event, Ms. Wynn-Williams said nothing about Meta or her book.” Meta, according to the lawsuit, even took issue with Wynn-Williams attending an arts and literary festival in the U.K. earlier this year, where she sat on a panel but remained silent — because other panelists were critics of the company. “Meta is pursuing Ms. Wynn-Williams at the expense of free speech and legal constraints not only because she refused to bow to the greed and power of Meta, Mr. Zuckerberg, and other executives, but also to strike fear into the heart of anyone else who dares to consider speaking the truth about Meta’s unlawful and abusive practices in the public interest,” the lawsuit says.

headlines.title: Mayor Daniel Lurie officiates Pride weddings in San Francisco
headlines.description: San Francisco has turned into the City of Love for Pride weekend. On Friday morning, hundreds of couples filled City Hall for a special Pride wedding ceremony. San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie officiated several ceremonies, with couples receiving commemorative Pride marriage licenses and posing for photos at a special booth inside City Hall. Couples said the day was about celebrating their love in a city with a long history of supporting and celebrating the LGBTQ+ community. “I mean, San Francisco, historically, has been known as the safe haven for the LGBTQ community, so I’m just so honored to be on the Pride Day weekend, Pride weekend, and yeah, just be here and celebrating our love,” Ted Huang said. More than 250 couples are scheduled to tie the knot Friday at City Hall.

headlines.title: Newsom urges a national ‘billionaires' tax' while fighting one in California
headlines.description: California Gov. Gavin Newsom, a Democrat who is considering a run for president as he approaches the end of his term, called for a national “billionaires’ tax” on Friday even as he fights another proposal targeting the wealthy in his home state. Newsom also said the U.S. government should own a stake in artificial intelligence companies. His proposals, outlined in a Substack post, aligns him with the Democratic Party’s populist left, and he argued that urgent changes are needed to prevent the elite concentration of wealth and power from undermining democracy. “It’s time for an economic reset for America,” Newsom wrote. The governor announced his agenda a day after an influential health care union in California pledged to go forward with a ballot measure that would impose a one-time 5% tax on the assets of billionaires living in the state as of Jan. 1, 2026. Newsom opposes that measure, as do many of the liberal interest groups that typically favor higher taxes. They fear it would drive billionaires out of California, eroding the state’s tax base over the long term for a one-time influx of cash. A technology mecca, California has more billionaires than any other state — a few hundred, by some estimates. “You may not be able to pick up and move to Texas or Florida to shelter your income from taxation, but I promise you that billionaires can, and do,” Newsom wrote. “Wealth is movable, and it shops for the state with the lowest taxes. The fight belongs at the federal level, where this broken system was created in the first place.” A minimum tax on large net worths Newsom said the solution is a new national tax policy, rather than a state-by-state system. He proposed a minimum tax on anyone with a net worth above $100 million. He also wants to make it illegal for the wealthy to borrow against their stock portfolios to fund their luxury lifestyles tax free. Newsom said there should be new rules for inheritance taxes, warning that “the transfer of wealth among the ultra-wealthy will lock in a permanent American aristocracy of inherited wealth.” And he wants to raise corporate tax rates to where they were before President Donald Trump’s first-term tax cut. The need is especially urgent as artificial intelligence threatens to displace workers and further concentrate wealth, he wrote. “We need to ensure every American owns a stake in the future being built by AI through a national public equity fund that takes a major stake in the new economy,” he wrote. “Simply, as artificial intelligence reshapes the country, every American should own a piece of the future it builds.” Revenue generated by his proposals could be used to retrain workers, fund universal child care, make college free and increase funding for health care. ‘Money buys influence’ Newsom, who has drawn attention as one of Trump’s most high-profile political antagonists, is getting an early start on laying out a policy framework for his potential White House bid months before the midterm elections, which have typically marked the informal start of overt presidential campaigning. The embrace of a wealth tax by Newsom, a moderate on tax policy despite his liberal reputation, signals a notable shift in the political landscape since Massachusetts Sen. Elizabeth Warren struggled to get traction in her 2020 campaign, which she largely centered around a 2% wealth tax. Newsom portrayed the nation’s tax code as a corrupt system built to help an elite few. “Money buys influence, and influence rewrites the rules,” he wrote. “Those rewritten rules funnel even more wealth to the few. Under this weight, democracy itself starts to buckle.” U.S. Rep. Ro Khanna, a Democrat from Silicon Valley who is also considering running for president, said Newsom is trying to duck the California fight with a national proposal that goes too easy on billionaires. He supports the California ballot measure. “It’s not going to pass muster to say, ‘Well, when we were fighting to have a billionaire tax to save healthcare for 3 million Californians, I sided with the billionaires, but in the future, I want to tax these billionaires,’” Khanna told reporters.
headlines.source: Jonathan J. Cooper | The Associated Press
